entities.summary: The orator, statesman, and philosopher (106–43 BC); author of the corpus. Consul 63 BC.
Cicero's younger brother (102–43 BC), praetor 62 BC, governor of Asia 61–58 BC, legate of Caesar in Gaul.
Cicero's lifelong intimate (110–32 BC), wealthy Epicurean banker, recipient of the Ad Atticum letters; later Q. Cicero's brother-in-law.
Cicero's first wife (c. 98–c. 6 BC), of independent property; mother of Tullia and Marcus the younger; divorced 46 BC.
Cicero's beloved daughter (c. 79–45 BC); married three times (Piso, Crassipes, Dolabella); died after childbirth in February 45 BC, an event that crushed Cicero.
Cicero's son (65 BC – c. 25 BC); studied at Athens; a tribune under Brutus and later consul under Augustus 30 BC.
The leading orator of his generation before Cicero (114–50 BC); consul 69 BC. The opposing counsel in Pro Quinctio and the prosecutor in In Verrem; later a friend.
The conqueror of Gaul, dictator, and adversary of the Republic (100–44 BC); consul 59 BC; Cicero's patron in 56 BC after Luca; assassinated on the Ides of March.
The general (106–48 BC); consul 70, 55, 52 BC; conqueror of Mithridates; member of the First Triumvirate; defeated at Pharsalus and murdered in Egypt.
The wealthiest Roman of his age (115–53 BC); consul 70, 55 BC; suppressor of Spartacus; member of the First Triumvirate; killed at Carrhae.
The Stoic statesman (95–46 BC), great-grandson of the Censor; tribune 62 BC; champion of senatorial constitutionalism; suicide at Utica after Pharsalus.
The popularis tribune (c. 93–52 BC) whose 58 BC bill drove Cicero into exile; a patrician adopted into a plebeian family to qualify for the tribunate; killed by Milo's gang on the Appian Way.
The patrician conspirator (c. 108–62 BC) whose plot against the consular government in 63 BC Cicero exposed and crushed; killed at the battle of Pistoria in January 62 BC.
Praetor and propraetor of Sicily 73–71 BC; prosecuted by Cicero for extortion in 70 BC; withdrew into exile after the first action.
Tribune 57 BC; champion of Cicero's recall from exile; killed Clodius in the affray on the Appian Way in 52 BC; defended (unsuccessfully) by Cicero in Pro Milone.
Consul 44 BC, triumvir 43 BC; target of the fourteen Philippics; Cicero's death warrant came from him after the proscriptions of November 43 BC.
The tyrannicide (85–42 BC); praetor 44 BC; one of the principal assassins of Caesar; recipient of the Ad Brutum letters; defeated and dead at Philippi.
Caesarian, consul 44 BC, married to Tullia 50 BC. Charming and impossible; a constant source of correspondence-borne anxiety to Cicero.
Consul 60 BC; husband of Clodia (Catullus's Lesbia); died suddenly in 59 BC.
The capital. Almost every speech is delivered here; almost every letter is sent from or to here.
The civic and judicial heart of Rome; site of the Rostra, the senatorial Curia, the basilicas where Cicero pleads.
The chamber where the Senate met; on the north side of the Forum.
The chief hill of Rome, site of the temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus, where the consuls take vows on the Kalends of January.
Capital of Macedonia; Cicero's residence for most of his exile in 58 BC, in the household of the quaestor Plancius.
Adriatic port city of Epirus, terminus of the Via Egnatia; Cicero's residence in late 58 BC and the city he sailed from on returning from exile.
The principal port of southern Italy on the Adriatic; the embarkation and return point for Cicero's exile crossings.
Coastal town in southern Latium; site of one of Cicero's villas, and the place of his murder in December 43 BC.
Hill town near Rome; site of Cicero's favourite villa and the setting of the Tusculan Disputations.
Coastal town south of Rome; site of one of Cicero's villas; setting of several Atticus letters.
Head of the Roman state; two were elected annually, gave their names to the year, commanded armies, and presided over the Senate.
Annually-elected magistrate ranking below consul; presided over the standing courts and (as praetor urbanus) issued the annual edict that governed civil procedure.
Magistrate of the plebs (ten elected annually); could veto magistrates and senatorial decrees and convoke the plebeian assembly.
Junior magistracy concerned with the city, the markets, and the public games; a stepping-stone to the praetorship.
Most junior of the regular magistracies; financial officers attached to the consuls and provincial governors.
The deliberative body of the Roman state, composed of ex-magistrates; addressed by Cicero as patres conscripti. Issued senatus consulta which had moral but not legal force.
The sovereign assembled body of citizens; legislator and elector, addressed by Cicero from the Rostra as Quirites.
Chief Roman priestly college, headed by the pontifex maximus; jurisdiction over sacral law, including consecration of property.
Chief god of the Roman state, with his temple on the Capitol; addressed by consul Cicero in the Catilinarians.
Goddess of the hearth; her cult was tended by the Vestal Virgins at the temple in the Forum.
The defendant in Cicero's first surviving speech (81 BC); brother and heir of Gaius Quinctius; embroiled in a partnership dispute with Sextus Naevius.
Brother of Publius Quinctius; partner with Sextus Naevius in a Gallic cattle and farming business; died in Gaul before the events of the speech.
The antagonist in Pro Quinctio; a sometime auctioneer turned partner of Gaius Quinctius in Gaul; the speech accuses him of attempting to seize P. Quinctius's goods by procedural fraud.
The praetor and jurist (d. c. 44 BC) presiding as iudex in Pro Quinctio; a celebrated authority on civil law, author of the actio de dolo malo.
Quinctius's former advocate, absent on a fresh embassy when Pro Quinctio was delivered; Cicero replaced him at short notice.
Quinctius's procurator at Rome and a relation of Sextus Naevius; tore down Naevius's notice of attachment and retook a slave Naevius had seized.
Common acquaintance of Naevius and Quinctius, named by Naevius as his arbiter in the partnership dispute.
Quinctius's travel companion to Gaul on 29 January 83 BC, named in the speech as a respectable witness to the date of departure.
An associate of Naevius bringing slaves for sale from Gaul; sighted Quinctius at Vada Volaterrana and reported it to Naevius, triggering the procedural ambush.
Praetor at Rome at the time Naevius applied to attach Quinctius's goods; his order to advertise the sale is the procedural anchor of the speech.
The praetor (NOT P. Cornelius Dolabella the consul) before whom Naevius later sought to compel the sponsio that became the form of action in Pro Quinctio.
Creditor of Gaius Quinctius; on his death the debt to his children had to be settled by Publius Quinctius, the Scapula children handled by Aquilius's good offices.
Roman commander in Gaul at the time of the events of Pro Quinctio (83 BC); Quinctius took refuge with him after being driven from the partnership land.

The Roman province (Cisalpine, Transalpine, or both depending on context). In Pro Quinctio: the partnership's pasture land and Naevius's auction site; in the Caesarian letters: the theatre of the Gallic War.
Roman colony in Transalpine Gaul (founded 118 BC); in Pro Quinctio, the proposed site for an auction Quinctius was dissuaded from holding.
Coastal location in Etruria; the place where Quinctius was sighted by Publicius travelling to Gaul on 29 January 83 BC.
Temple in the Forum, used in Cicero's day for public business and as a checking point for treasury account-balances; mentioned in Pro Quinctio §17.
Auction halls at Rome; Cicero invokes them in Pro Quinctio as the milieu of Naevius the auctioneer.
A money-changer's table or bank in the Forum; the spot where Naevius staged his witness-testifying that Quinctius had not appeared.
Legally-authorised agent or attorney for an absent principal; central to Pro Quinctio, where Sex. Alfenus stands in for the absent Quinctius.
The annual edict in which each praetor laid out the formulae and remedies he would grant in his year of office; the framework of Roman civil procedure.
The Roman state treasury, housed in the temple of Saturn; under quaestorian custody.
The dictator (138–78 BC); marched on Rome twice; victor of the civil war of 83–82 BC; abdicated 79 BC. The proscriptions of his regime are the political background of Pro Roscio Amerino.
Sulla's most influential freedman, in charge of the proscription auctions; the unnamed power behind the prosecution in Pro Roscio Amerino.
Defendant in Pro Roscio Amerino; son of the murdered Sex. Roscius the elder; charged with parricide.
Murdered father in Pro Roscio Amerino; first man of Ameria, friend of the Metelli, the Servilii, the Scipios; killed near the Pallacinian baths in 81 BC.
Cousin of the murdered Sex. Roscius; named by Cicero as one of the actual murderers and conspirators with Chrysogonus.
Cousin of the murdered Sex. Roscius; named by Cicero as one of the murderers and conspirators with Chrysogonus.
Prosecutor in Pro Roscio Amerino; Cicero treats him with deliberate contempt as a hireling of the Roscii.
Roman noblewoman, daughter of Q. Caecilius Metellus Balearicus; sheltered Sex. Roscius the younger after his father's murder.
Young noble who took up Roscius's cause and brought it to court; later consul 61 BC.
Umbrian municipality; home of the Roscii in the case of Pro Roscio Amerino.
Etruscan town; site of Sulla's camp during the closing campaign of the civil war (81 BC).

Lists of citizens declared outlaw under Sulla in 82–81 BC; their lives forfeit and their property auctioned to the dictator's followers. Pro Roscio Amerino is the first oblique attack on the proscriptions in surviving Latin literature.
The most celebrated Roman comic actor of his day (d. c. 62 BC); a friend of Cicero, who acknowledged learning oratorical delivery from him; defendant in Pro Roscio Comoedo.
Prosecutor in Pro Roscio Comoedo; suing Roscius the actor over a partnership in the slave-actor Panurgus.
Judge addressed by Cicero in Pro Roscio Comoedo; consul 67 BC.
Plaintiff in Pro Tullio (NOT the orator); his slaves and pasture were attacked by armed slaves of P. Fabius.
Defendant in Pro Tullio; admitted (through his counsel L. Quinctius) that his armed slaves had attacked the plaintiff's establishment.
Counsel for the defence in Pro Tullio; tribune 74 BC, an opponent of senatorial control of the courts.
Brother of L. Lucullus; praetor 76 BC, instituted the formula for damnum vi armata datum which is the form of action in Pro Tullio; consul 73 BC.
Civil action for damage done by armed force, instituted by M. Lucullus as praetor in 76 BC; the form of action in Pro Tullio.
King of Syracuse (270–215 BC); a steady ally of Rome through the Punic Wars; framer of the lex Hieronica that governed Sicilian grain taxation under the Roman province.
Capital of Roman Sicily; centre of Verres's depredations; site of the Achradina executions and the Latomiae quarry-prison in In Verrem II.5.
City on the strait between Sicily and Italy; Verres's collaborators' city, and the site of the cross facing Italy on which Cicero's Roman citizen Publius Gavius was hung.
Sicilian hill-town of the Ceres cult, in the centre of the island; site of the rape of Proserpine in myth, and of Verres's stripping of the temple of Ceres in In Verrem II.4.
Sicilian city on the south coast; site of the temple of Hercules whose statue Verres tried to remove in In Verrem II.4.
Sicilian city in the west; site of the bronze Diana whose theft by Verres is one of the famous narrative passages of In Verrem II.4.
Wealthy and respected citizen of Messana; his shrine of Eros, a small Cupid by Praxiteles, and a Hercules by Myron were taken by Verres in 73 BC; the principal opening victim of In Verrem II.4.
Citizen of Thermae in Sicily; his trial in absentia by Verres on a fabricated capital charge is the central narrative of In Verrem II.2.
Syracusan put in command of the Sicilian fleet by Verres in 71 BC; his abandonment of the fleet at Helorus and the resulting loss are the spine of In Verrem II.5.
Roman citizen of Consa whom Verres had crucified at Messana facing Italy; his cry "civis Romanus sum" is the architectural climax of In Verrem II.5.
Verres's bagman in the Sicilian grain extortions; the principal villain of In Verrem II.3.
Sicilian grain-tithe law inherited by Rome from the kingdom of Hiero II of Syracuse; framework for the tithe-farming Verres allegedly broke; central to In Verrem II.3.
First Roman province (from 241 BC); subject of In Verrem; its cities supplied much of Rome's grain.
Defendant in Pro Caecina (69 BC); Etruscan landowner whose interdictum de vi armata claim against Aebutius is the form of action.
Former praetorian governor of Transalpine Gaul (76–73 BC); defendant in Pro Fonteio (69 BC), prosecuted for extortion by an embassy of Gauls under Indutiomarus.
Defendant in Pro Cluentio (66 BC); accused of poisoning his stepfather Oppianicus; Cicero's defence is the longest of the forensic speeches and turns on the eight-year shadow of the Junian trial.
Aged senator defended by Cicero in Pro Rabirio Perduellionis Reo (63 BC), revived charge of perduellio for the killing of the tribune L. Saturninus 37 years earlier in 100 BC.
Tribune 103 and 100 BC; killed by senatorial action under the senatus consultum ultimum in 100 BC, the precedent revived against Rabirius in 63 BC.
Tribune 63 BC; proposer of the agrarian bill against which Cicero spoke the three De Lege Agraria speeches.
Praetor 63 BC, Catilinarian conspirator, executed in the Tullianum on Cicero's orders 5 December 63 BC.
Catilinarian conspirator; possessor of the cache of swords in his house; executed with Lentulus 5 December 63 BC.
Consul-elect for 62 BC at the time of the Catilinarian debate; opened the senatorial discussion on 5 December 63 BC voting for the death penalty for the conspirators.
Consul-elect for 62 BC; defendant in Pro Murena (late November 63 BC) on charges of canvassing brought under Cicero's own bribery law.
Underground state prison near the Forum; place of execution by strangling for state criminals; site of the killing of Lentulus, Cethegus, and the other Catilinarians on the night of 5 December 63 BC.
Temple at the foot of the Palatine, near the start of the Sacra Via; site of the Senate meeting of 8 November 63 BC at which Cicero delivered the First Catilinarian.
Temple at the foot of the Capitoline beside the Forum; site of the Senate meeting of 5 December 63 BC at which Cicero delivered the Fourth Catilinarian.

aliases: senatus consultum de re publica defendenda
entities.summary: Senatorial decree commanding the consuls to see that the commonwealth take no harm; first issued in 121 BC against C. Gracchus, then in 100 BC against Saturninus, then in 63 BC against Catiline. Authorised use of force; its constitutional standing was challenged repeatedly by populares.
Greek poet of Antioch; client of the Lucullus family; defendant in Pro Archia (62 BC) in a citizenship-status case. Cicero's defence is the famous panegyric of literary culture.
Nephew of the dictator; convicted of electoral bribery in 66 BC, defended by Cicero in Pro Sulla (62 BC) on a charge of complicity in the Catilinarian conspiracy.
Praetor 63 BC, propraetor of Asia 62 BC; defendant in Pro Flacco (mid-59 BC) on extortion charges; one of Cicero's allies during the Catilinarian crisis.
Tribune 57 BC; champion of Cicero's recall from exile; defendant in Pro Sestio (March 56 BC) on charges of public violence; acquitted unanimously.
Tribune 59 BC; Caesarian; cross-examined by Cicero in In Vatinium (March 56 BC) as a hostile witness in the Sestius trial.
Younger orator and politician; Cicero's protege; defendant in Pro Caelio (4 April 56 BC), the famous Clodia speech; later a correspondent in the Ad Familiares.
Sister of P. Clodius and widow of Q. Metellus Celer; the (probable) Lesbia of Catullus; the unnamed accuser behind the prosecution in Pro Caelio.

Tribune 67 BC (proposer of the lex Gabinia for Pompey's pirate command), consul 58 BC; one of the consuls under whom Cicero went into exile, hence one of the "two whirlwinds of the commonwealth" of Pro Sestio.
Consul 58 BC, father-in-law of Caesar; the other of the "two whirlwinds" of Pro Sestio (with Gabinius); proconsul of Macedonia and target of In Pisonem (55 BC).
Consul 57 BC; champion of Cicero's recall from exile; recipient of the long Egyptian-question correspondence in Ad Familiares book 1 (early 56 BC).
Consul 74 BC; commander in the Mithridatic War; brought Archias to Rome under his patronage; great-grandfather of the Lucullan luxury tradition.
Roman epic poet (239--169 BC); author of the Annales (the first Roman national epic in hexameters); revered by Cicero as the great early Latin poet, frequently quoted in the corpus.
The Greek poet of the Iliad and Odyssey; the literary touchstone for Cicero, especially in the letters where Greek tags from Homer appear constantly.
Greek federate city in Lucania; the city in whose roll Archias was enrolled, the basis of his claim to Roman citizenship under the lex Plautia Papiria.
Capital of Seleucid Syria; birthplace of the poet Archias; centre of Greek culture in the eastern Mediterranean.
Young prosecutor in Pro Caelio (56 BC); son of L. Calpurnius Bestia, whom Caelius had himself prosecuted earlier in 56 BC.
Head of the Egyptian embassy of 57 BC opposing Ptolemy XII Auletes; murdered in Rome under suspicious circumstances. Caelius was charged with complicity in the murder.
Tribune; prosecuted by Caelius earlier in 56 BC; father of the prosecutor Atratinus in Pro Caelio.
